solar.hint: Our Solar System. Click any object to learn about it. Use the scale slider to see how empty space really is.

sun.name: The Sun
sun.caption: A medium-sized yellow star at the centre of our Solar System. Its gravity holds all the planets in orbit.

mercury.name: Mercury
mercury.caption: The smallest planet and the closest to the Sun. It has almost no atmosphere, so it bakes by day and freezes at night.

venus.name: Venus
venus.caption: Earth's 'evil twin'. A thick CO₂ atmosphere traps heat, making it the hottest planet — even hotter than Mercury.

earth.name: Earth
earth.caption: The only planet known to have life. Liquid water, a protective atmosphere and a magnetic field make it special.

moon.name: The Moon (Luna)
moon.caption: Earth's only natural satellite. Its gravity causes our ocean tides. The same side always faces Earth.

mars.name: Mars
mars.caption: The 'Red Planet'. Its colour comes from iron oxide (rust) on the surface. Has the tallest volcano in the Solar System.

jupiter.name: Jupiter
jupiter.caption: The biggest planet — a giant ball of gas with a storm (the Great Red Spot) larger than Earth that has lasted centuries.

saturn.name: Saturn
saturn.caption: Famous for its spectacular rings made of ice and rock. Saturn is so light it would float in water!

uranus.name: Uranus
uranus.caption: An 'ice giant' that rotates on its side. A long-ago collision is thought to have knocked it over.

neptune.name: Neptune
neptune.caption: The windiest planet — supersonic winds rip through its blue atmosphere at over 2,000 km/h.

asteroid.name: Asteroid Belt
asteroid.caption: Rocky leftovers from the Solar System's formation, mostly between Mars and Jupiter. Smaller pieces that fly through space are called meteoroids.

comet.name: Comet
comet.caption: A 'dirty snowball' of ice, dust and rock. When it nears the Sun, it heats up and grows a glowing tail that always points away from the Sun.

facts.Tail: Up to millions of km long

meteor.name: Meteor (Shooting Star)
meteor.caption: A meteoroid burning up in Earth's atmosphere from friction. If a piece reaches the ground it's called a meteorite.

stars.hint: The Sun and all 8 planets, side by side at relative size. Click any for facts.

bodies.name: Sun
bodies.caption: Our star. About 109 Earths could fit across its diameter.

bodies.name: Mercury
bodies.caption: Smallest planet, closest to the Sun. Almost no atmosphere.

bodies.name: Venus
bodies.caption: Earth's 'evil twin' — hottest planet, thick CO₂ atmosphere traps heat.

bodies.name: Earth
bodies.caption: Our home. The only planet known to have life.

bodies.name: Mars
bodies.caption: The 'Red Planet'. Rust-coloured soil, tallest volcano in the Solar System.

bodies.name: Jupiter
bodies.caption: Biggest planet — about 11× Earth's diameter. Storm bigger than Earth has raged for centuries.

bodies.name: Saturn
bodies.caption: Famous for its rings of ice and rock. So light it would float in water!

bodies.name: Uranus
bodies.caption: An ice giant that rotates on its side. Pale blue-green from methane in its atmosphere.

bodies.name: Neptune
bodies.caption: The windiest planet — supersonic winds up to 2,100 km/h.

remnants.hint: When stars die, they leave behind some of the strangest objects in the universe.

white-dwarf.name: White Dwarf
white-dwarf.caption: The hot, dense core left behind when a Sun-like star dies. Earth-sized but as heavy as the Sun.

neutron-star.name: Neutron Star
neutron-star.caption: What's left after a massive star explodes. A city-sized ball so dense that one teaspoon would weigh as much as a mountain.

pulsar.name: Pulsar
pulsar.caption: A spinning neutron star that beams radio waves out of its poles. Like a cosmic lighthouse, we see a pulse every time the beam sweeps past Earth.

black-hole.name: Black Hole
black-hole.caption: An object with gravity so strong that not even light can escape. The bright ring around it is an accretion disk — superheated gas spiralling in.

supernova.name: Supernova
supernova.caption: The gigantic explosion of a dying massive star. Briefly outshines an entire galaxy and scatters elements like iron and gold across space.

galaxies.hint: Galaxies are vast collections of stars, gas, dust and dark matter held together by gravity. There are over 2 trillion in the observable universe.

milky-way.name: Milky Way
milky-way.caption: Our home galaxy. A barred spiral containing 100–400 billion stars. Our Sun is on one of the spiral arms.

andromeda.name: Andromeda
andromeda.caption: Our closest large galactic neighbour. It's heading toward us — in about 4.5 billion years it will collide with the Milky Way.

elliptical.name: Elliptical Galaxy
elliptical.caption: Football-shaped galaxies made of mostly older stars, with little gas left to form new ones. Often the largest galaxies in the universe.

irregular.name: Irregular Galaxy
irregular.caption: Galaxies with no clear shape, often warped by collisions with other galaxies. Many small galaxies are irregular.

constellations.hint: The 12 zodiac constellations — the patterns the Sun appears to pass through over the year. Click any to fly closer.

aries.name: Aries ♈ (The Ram)
aries.caption: A small, faint constellation. In Greek myth, this is the ram with the Golden Fleece. Brightest star is Hamal.

taurus.name: Taurus ♉ (The Bull)
taurus.caption: Easy to spot by its V-shaped face and the orange eye — Aldebaran, a red giant. Contains the Pleiades star cluster.

gemini.name: Gemini ♊ (The Twins)
gemini.caption: Two long parallel lines of stars topped by Castor and Pollux — the heads of the mythological twins.

cancer.name: Cancer ♋ (The Crab)
cancer.caption: The faintest zodiac constellation. Contains the Beehive Cluster (M44) — a nice binocular target.

leo.name: Leo ♌ (The Lion)
leo.caption: One of the few constellations that actually looks like its namesake. The 'sickle' forms the lion's mane.

virgo.name: Virgo ♍ (The Maiden)
virgo.caption: The second-largest constellation. Brightest star is Spica, a hot blue-white giant.

libra.name: Libra ♎ (The Scales)
libra.caption: The only zodiac constellation that represents an object, not a person or animal. Was originally the claws of Scorpius.

scorpius.name: Scorpius ♏ (The Scorpion)
scorpius.caption: Bright, scorpion-shaped pattern with the red supergiant Antares as its heart. Excellent view from WA in winter.

sagittarius.name: Sagittarius ♐ (The Archer)
sagittarius.caption: Look for the 'teapot' asterism. The centre of our Milky Way galaxy lies in this direction.

capricorn.name: Capricorn ♑ (The Sea-goat)
capricorn.caption: A mythological creature with a goat's body and fish's tail. Faint but ancient — one of the oldest recognised patterns.

aquarius.name: Aquarius ♒ (The Water-bearer)
aquarius.caption: A large but faint pattern showing a person pouring water. Best viewed under dark skies away from city lights.

pisces.name: Pisces ♓ (The Fish)
pisces.caption: Two fish connected by a long cord — in myth, Aphrodite and her son tied together as they fled. Mostly faint stars.

celestialBodies.title: Celestial Bodies
celestialBodies.hint: The eight kinds of objects you'll find in space. Click any card to read more.
learningIntentions: I can describe in my own words what a planet, star, moon, asteroid, meteor, comet, constellation and galaxy are.
I can explain how each of these is different from the others.

items.name: Planets
items.description: A planet is a large round object that orbits a star (like our Sun). Planets are not stars — they don't make their own light. They shine because they reflect the light from their star. Our Solar System has eight planets, divided into rocky terrestrial planets close to the Sun and bigger gas and ice giants further out.
items.examples: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune

items.name: Stars
items.description: A star is a huge ball of hot gas (mostly hydrogen and helium) held together by gravity. Stars produce their own light and heat through a process called nuclear fusion in their cores. Our Sun is a medium-sized star — there are stars much smaller and much bigger.
items.examples: The Sun, Sirius, Betelgeuse, Proxima Centauri

items.name: Moons
items.description: A moon is a natural object that orbits a planet. Moons are smaller than the planet they orbit. Earth has one moon, but Jupiter has more than 90! Moons are also called natural satellites because they go around their planet, just like a planet goes around its star.
items.examples: Earth's Moon (Luna), Jupiter's Io and Europa, Saturn's Titan

items.name: Asteroids
items.description: An asteroid is a rocky leftover from when the Solar System formed about 4.6 billion years ago. Most asteroids are found in the asteroid belt, a wide ring of rocks between Mars and Jupiter. They are too small to be planets — most are less than 1,000 km across.
items.examples: Ceres (the largest, 940 km wide), Vesta, Pallas

items.name: Meteors
items.description: A meteor is the bright streak of light you see when a small piece of space rock or dust burns up in Earth's atmosphere. The piece of rock while it's still in space is called a meteoroid. If a piece survives the fall and hits the ground, it's called a meteorite.
items.examples: Shooting stars, the Perseid meteor shower in August

items.name: Comets
items.description: A comet is like a 'dirty snowball' made of ice, dust and rock. Comets orbit the Sun on very long, stretched-out paths. When a comet gets close to the Sun, the ice heats up and turns into gas, forming a glowing tail that always points away from the Sun.
items.examples: Halley's Comet (returns every 76 years), Comet NEOWISE

items.name: Constellations
items.description: A constellation is a pattern of stars in the night sky. The stars in a constellation are usually not actually close together — they just look that way from Earth. Astronomers have agreed on 88 official constellations. The 12 that the Sun appears to move through each year make up the zodiac.

items.name: Galaxies
items.description: A galaxy is a huge collection of stars, gas, dust and dark matter all held together by gravity. Our galaxy is called the Milky Way and contains over 100 billion stars. Astronomers think there are more than 2 trillion galaxies in the observable universe.
items.examples: Milky Way (ours), Andromeda, Sombrero Galaxy, Whirlpool Galaxy

comparisons.q: What is the difference between a meteor and a meteorite?
comparisons.a: A meteor is the streak of light in the sky as a rock burns up in the atmosphere. A meteorite is the piece of rock that survives and lands on the ground.

comparisons.q: What is the difference between an asteroid and a comet?
comparisons.a: Asteroids are made of rock and stay mostly in the asteroid belt. Comets are made of ice and dust and grow a glowing tail when they get close to the Sun.

comparisons.q: What is the difference between a star and a planet?
comparisons.a: Stars make their own light by nuclear fusion. Planets do not — they only shine because they reflect light from a star.

comparisons.q: What is the difference between a moon and a planet?
comparisons.a: A planet orbits a star. A moon orbits a planet. Moons are usually smaller than the planet they orbit.

comparisons.q: What is the difference between a constellation and a galaxy?
comparisons.a: A constellation is just a pattern of stars we see from Earth — they aren't really grouped together in space. A galaxy is an actual huge group of stars, gas and dust held together by gravity.

gallery.title: Pillars of Creation
gallery.caption: Towers of gas and dust in the Eagle Nebula where new stars are being born. The Webb telescope's infrared view sees through dust the Hubble couldn't, revealing thousands of young stars.

gallery.title: Cosmic Cliffs in the Carina Nebula
gallery.caption: What looks like mountain peaks is actually the edge of a giant gas cloud about 7,600 light-years away. The 'peaks' are about 7 light-years tall.

gallery.title: Stephan's Quintet
gallery.caption: Five galaxies in a tight group — four of them are interacting and pulling each other out of shape as they slowly collide.

gallery.title: Southern Ring Nebula
gallery.caption: Clouds of gas and dust thrown off by a dying star. Two views from Webb's different cameras side-by-side reveal a previously hidden second star at the centre.

gallery.title: Rho Ophiuchi Cloud Complex
gallery.caption: The closest star-forming region to Earth, about 390 light-years away. The red jets are gas ejected by newborn stars slamming into surrounding clouds.

gallery.title: Uranus and its Rings
gallery.caption: Webb's infrared view shows Uranus's faint rings — almost invisible in normal photos — and the bright polar cap caused by sunlight hitting its sideways-tilted pole.

gallery.title: Andromeda Galaxy — Sharpest View
gallery.caption: The largest Hubble image ever — 1.5 billion pixels — showing our nearest large galactic neighbour, the Andromeda Galaxy (M31), in incredible detail.

gallery.title: Stellar Spire in the Eagle Nebula
gallery.caption: A 9.5-light-year-tall tower of cold gas and dust being eroded by ultraviolet light from nearby young stars. New stars are forming inside the spire.

gallery.title: Lagoon Nebula
gallery.caption: A giant cloud where stars are being born, about 4,000 light-years from Earth. Hubble's 28th-anniversary image, showing turbulent gas lit up by a massive young star.

gallery.title: Sombrero Galaxy
gallery.caption: An edge-on spiral galaxy 28 million light-years away. The dark band is a ring of dust circling the galaxy's bright central bulge.

gallery.title: Westerlund 2 — Hubble's 25th Anniversary
gallery.caption: A young star cluster, about 20,000 light-years away, blazing inside a vast cloud where new stars are still being formed.

gallery.title: Arp 273 — A Rose Made of Galaxies
gallery.caption: Two galaxies caught in a slow gravitational dance, pulled into a rose-like shape by the larger one's gravity.

gallery.title: Cartwheel Galaxy
gallery.caption: A wheel-shaped galaxy 500 million light-years away, created when a smaller galaxy punched straight through its centre, sending shockwaves outward.

gallery.title: NGC 604 — Star-forming Region
gallery.caption: An immense star-forming region in a nearby galaxy, lit up by more than 200 massive young stars hidden inside the gas clouds.

gallery.title: Horsehead Nebula
gallery.caption: One of the most recognisable shapes in the sky — a column of cold gas about 1,300 light-years away, sculpted by radiation from nearby young stars.

gallery.title: Bubble Nebula
gallery.caption: Hubble's 26th-anniversary image — a giant bubble of gas seven light-years across, being blown into space by a hot massive star at its centre.

gallery.title: Crab Nebula
gallery.caption: The shattered remains of a star that Chinese astronomers saw explode in 1054 AD. A pulsar spins at its centre 30 times every second.

gallery.title: Whirlpool Galaxy (M51)
gallery.caption: A grand spiral galaxy 23 million light-years away, locked in a slow gravitational dance with a smaller companion at its side.

gallery.title: Tarantula Nebula
gallery.caption: The largest known star-forming region in our cosmic neighbourhood, in the Large Magellanic Cloud — visible to the naked eye from the Southern Hemisphere.

gallery.title: Cosmic Reef (NGC 2014 + NGC 2020)
gallery.caption: Hubble's 30th-anniversary image. Two glowing nebulae in the Large Magellanic Cloud, dramatically lit by hot young stars.

gallery.title: Mystic Mountain (Carina Nebula)
gallery.caption: A three-light-year-tall pillar of gas and dust being eroded by ultraviolet light from nearby young stars. Released for Hubble's 20th anniversary.

gallery.title: Veil Nebula
gallery.caption: The expanding shell of a star that exploded around 8,000 years ago. Glowing wisps span six full Moon widths across our sky.

gallery.title: Hubble Deep Field
gallery.caption: A patch of sky no bigger than a grain of sand held at arm's length — yet filled with thousands of galaxies, each containing billions of stars.

gallery.title: NGC 1300 — Barred Spiral Galaxy
gallery.caption: A classic barred spiral galaxy 61 million light-years away, with a straight 'bar' of stars across its centre. Our own Milky Way has a similar bar.
